word_list.word: opportunity (never|seldom) knocks twice at any man's door
word_list.pronunciation: ɑːpɚtˈuːnɪɾi nˈɛvɚ sˈɛldəm nˈɑːks twˈaɪs æt ˌɛni mˈænz dˈoːɹ

senses.definition: used to imply that one should be ready to seize opportunities when they arise, as they may not come again

examples.x: I missed out on a great job opportunity because I didn't act quickly enough, but I learned that opportunity never knocks twice at any man's door.

word_list.word: when life gives you lemons, make lemonade
word_list.pronunciation: wˌɛn lˈaɪf ɡˈɪvz juː lˈɛmənz mˌeɪk lˌɛmənˈeɪd

senses.definition: used to imply that one should make the best of a difficult or unpleasant situation, and turn it into an opportunity for something positive

word_list.word: mill cannot grind with water that is past
word_list.pronunciation: mˈɪl kænˈɑːt ɡɹˈaɪnd wɪð wˈɔːɾɚ ðæt ɪz pˈæst

senses.definition: used to imply that opportunities are only valuable when acted upon in the present moment and cannot be regained once they have passed

word_list.word: take the goods the gods provide
word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk ðə ɡˈʊdz ðə ɡˈɑːdz pɹəvˈaɪd

senses.definition: used to imply that one should utilize and enjoy good fortune or advantageous circumstances when they are presented, rather than taking them for granted or letting them go to waste

examples.x: When opportunities come our way, we should take the goods the gods provide and make the most of them.

word_list.word: live for today for tomorrow never comes
word_list.pronunciation: lˈaɪv fɔːɹ tədˈeɪ fɔːɹ təmˈɔːɹoʊ nˈɛvɚ kˈʌmz

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of enjoying the present moment and not postponing happiness or fulfillment for an uncertain future

examples.x: I've learned to live for today for tomorrow never comes, taking risks and pursuing my dreams without fearing the unknown.

word_list.word: a flower blooms more than once
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ flˈaʊɚ blˈuːmz mˈoːɹ ðɐn wˈʌns

senses.definition: used to imply opportunities in life are not limited to just one chance, but can recur again and again, encouraging people to stay optimistic and hopeful

examples.x: The coach told his team that a flower blooms more than once to encourage them to keep practicing and striving for success, even after a defeat.

word_list.word: hoist your sail when the wind is fair
word_list.pronunciation: hˈɔɪst jʊɹ sˈeɪl wɛn ðə wˈɪnd ɪz fˈɛɹ

senses.definition: used to suggest that when conditions are favorable for success, one should act quickly to achieve their goals

examples.x: As a small business owner, you need to be ready to launch your new product when the market conditions are right, and hoist your sail when the wind is fair.

senses.definition: used to  imply that everyone deserves a fair chance or opportunity to succeed or experience something, regardless of their past behavior or reputation

examples.x: The coach gave the inexperienced player a chance to prove himself, saying every dog gets one bite to emphasize the importance of giving everyone a fair opportunity.

word_list.pronunciation: wɛn æz wˈʌn dˈoːɹ klˈoʊzᵻz ʃˈʌts ɐnˈʌðɚ wˈʌn ˈoʊpənz

senses.definition: used to suggest that when an opportunity is missed or a situation does not work out as planned, there will always be new opportunities or options that arise

examples.x: I was really disappointed when I didn't get the job I applied for, but I'm trying to stay positive and remember that when one door closes another opens.

word_list.word: one today is worth two tomorrows
word_list.pronunciation: wˈʌn tədˈeɪ ɪz wˈɜːθ tˈuː təmˈɔːɹoʊz

senses.definition: used to suggest that the present moment is more valuable than the future, as taking action now can lead to greater rewards and benefits in the long term

examples.x: I always remind myself that one today is worth two tomorrows, so I try to make the most of each day and not procrastinate.
